skill_name: Verb Complementation: Basic (Like/Love/Hate + -ing, Gerund as Subject/Complement)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My little brother hates ___ (eat) vegetables at dinner.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after hate is a common learner error mapping to L1 transfer; hate as a general preference verb requires -ing
bare base verb after hate is ungrammatical; a gerund suffix is required
third-person singular inflection produces a finite verb, which cannot function as a gerund object

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom loves ___ (play) football with his friends.
distractor_rationale: to + -ing is a double-marking error; only the gerund form without 'to' is correct here
bare base verb after loves is ungrammatical; the gerund suffix is required
past tense form cannot function as a gerund object after loves

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ (swim) in the sea is Anna's favourite activity in summer.
distractor_rationale: to + -ing is a double-marking error; only the gerund form without 'to' can function as a sentence subject
bare infinitive cannot function as a grammatical subject in this position without 'to'; and even 'to swim' is less natural here than the gerund
finite third-person verb form cannot function as a noun phrase subject

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria enjoys ___ (cook) new recipes at the weekend when she has more time.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after enjoy is a very common learner error; enjoy exclusively takes a gerund object
bare base verb after enjoys is ungrammatical; a gerund is required
past participle form cannot function as a gerund object after enjoy

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Do you mind ___ (wait) here for a few minutes while I get my coat?
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after mind is a common learner error; mind requires a gerund object, not an infinitive
bare base verb after mind is ungrammatical; the gerund suffix is required
past tense form cannot function as a gerund object after mind

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James always avoids ___ (take) the bus during rush hour because it is very crowded.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after avoid is a frequent learner error; avoid exclusively takes a gerund object
bare base verb after avoids is ungrammatical; a gerund is required
third-person singular finite form cannot serve as a gerund object after avoids

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: She prefers ___ (shop) online rather than going to a busy supermarket.
distractor_rationale: to + -ing is a double-marking error; prefer as a general preference verb takes a bare gerund
bare base verb after prefers is ungrammatical; a gerund suffix is required
past tense form cannot function as a gerund object after prefers

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David finally finished ___ (write) his report and sent it to his manager before lunchtime.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after finish is a common learner error; finish exclusively takes a gerund object
bare base verb after finished is ungrammatical; a gerund is required
past participle form cannot function as a gerund object after finished

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ (learn) a new language takes a lot of time and regular practice every day.
distractor_rationale: to + -ing is a double-marking error; only the gerund without 'to' can head a noun-phrase subject in this position
bare base verb cannot function as a noun-phrase subject; a gerund is required
past tense form cannot function as a noun-phrase subject

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Lisa doesn't enjoy ___ (present) her ideas in front of a large group of people at work.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after enjoy is a very common learner error; enjoy exclusively requires a gerund object
past tense form cannot function as a gerund object after enjoy
third-person singular finite form cannot serve as a gerund object after doesn't enjoy

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom loves ___ (swim) in the sea on hot days.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after 'loves' signals a specific wish ('would love to swim'), not a general preference, making it wrong in this habitual context
bare base form cannot directly follow 'loves' as a complement — a non-finite form is required
third-person present form creates two finite verbs in one clause without a conjunction, making the sentence ungrammatical

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna always avoids ___ (take) the bus during rush hour because it is too crowded.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is not used after 'avoid' — 'avoid' always requires a gerund complement
bare base form cannot follow 'avoids' as a complement — the gerund form is obligatory after this verb
past simple form is a finite verb and cannot function as the complement of 'avoids' in this structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ (cook) a big meal for the whole family every Sunday takes a lot of time and energy.
distractor_rationale: 'To cooking' combines 'to' with a gerund, which is not a valid English structure — the subject role here requires a gerund alone
bare base form 'Cook' cannot function as the grammatical subject of a sentence — a gerund is required for this noun-like subject role
past participle 'Cooked' cannot stand alone as the subject of a sentence without an auxiliary verb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Do you mind ___ (wait) for a few minutes while I finish this report for my manager?
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is not grammatically permitted after 'mind' — 'mind' always takes a gerund as its complement
bare base form cannot follow 'mind' as a complement — the gerund is the only correct non-finite form here
past simple form cannot function as the complement of 'mind' in this structure — it creates an ungrammatical sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: My little brother loves ___ football in the park.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is used after 'would love', not after 'loves' expressing general preference
bare base verb cannot follow 'loves' as a gerund object
past tense form cannot serve as a gerund complement after 'loves'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna hates ___ the dishes after dinner every night.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive follows 'would hate' for a specific event, not 'hates' expressing habitual dislike
bare base verb is ungrammatical directly after 'hates' as its complement
past tense form cannot function as a gerund object after 'hates'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ early every morning is very difficult for Tom.
distractor_rationale: 'To getting up' mixes to-infinitive and gerund, producing an impossible hybrid form
bare base verb phrase cannot function as the subject of a sentence; a gerund is required
third-person conjugated form cannot serve as the subject noun phrase of a clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: David enjoys ___ to new music on his way to work each morning.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is not used after 'enjoy'; 'enjoy' always requires a gerund complement
bare base verb is ungrammatical after 'enjoy' and does not express habitual activity
past tense form cannot act as a gerund object and changes the meaning incorrectly

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara would like ___ a cooking class next weekend with her sister.
distractor_rationale: 'would like' expresses a specific wish and requires to-infinitive, not a gerund
bare base verb cannot follow 'would like'; the to-infinitive marker is obligatory here
past tense form is ungrammatical after 'would like' and does not express a future wish

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Do you mind ___ the window? It's very hot in this room.
distractor_rationale: 'mind' always takes a gerund complement; to-infinitive after 'mind' is a very common learner error
bare base verb cannot directly follow 'mind' as its complement in standard grammar
past tense form is grammatically impossible as a gerund complement after 'mind'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James prefers ___ to work rather than taking the bus every day.
distractor_rationale: 'to cycling' incorrectly combines the preposition 'to' with a gerund after 'prefers'; the gerund alone is required
bare base verb is ungrammatical as a complement after 'prefers' without 'to'
past tense form cannot serve as a gerund object after 'prefers'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She always avoids ___ the supermarket on Saturday mornings because it gets very crowded.
distractor_rationale: 'avoid' always requires a gerund; using to-infinitive after 'avoid' is a common transfer error from learners' first languages
bare base verb is ungrammatical after 'avoid' and does not indicate habitual behaviour
third-person conjugated form cannot function as a gerund object after 'avoid'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark finished ___ the report just before the meeting started, so he felt very relieved.
distractor_rationale: 'finish' always takes a gerund complement; to-infinitive after 'finish' is a frequent learner error caused by overgeneralisation
bare base verb is ungrammatical directly after 'finished' as its object complement
past participle form cannot serve as a gerund object and confuses the verb phrase with a passive structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ a foreign language fluently takes a lot of practice and patience over many years.
distractor_rationale: 'To speaking' is an impossible hybrid of to-infinitive and gerund; only the gerund alone can function as the subject here
bare base verb cannot serve as the grammatical subject of a clause in standard English
past participle form cannot function as a gerund subject and suggests a passive meaning instead

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My brother enjoys to play football after school every day.
content.error_sentence: My brother enjoys to play football after school every day.
content.corrected_sentence: My brother enjoys playing football after school every day.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She hates get up early on cold winter mornings.
content.error_sentence: She hates get up early on cold winter mornings.
content.corrected_sentence: She hates getting up early on cold winter mornings.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom finished to write his homework before dinner and then watched TV.
content.error_sentence: Tom finished to write his homework before dinner and then watched TV.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom finished writing his homework before dinner and then watched TV.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Do you mind to wait here for a few minutes while I speak to the manager?
content.error_sentence: Do you mind to wait here for a few minutes while I speak to the manager?
content.corrected_sentence: Do you mind waiting here for a few minutes while I speak to the manager?

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Cook at home is much cheaper than eating at a restaurant every night.
content.error_sentence: Cook at home is much cheaper than eating at a restaurant every night.
content.corrected_sentence: Cooking at home is much cheaper than eating at a restaurant every night.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa prefers to walk to work, but she avoids to cross the busy main road.
content.error_sentence: Lisa prefers to walk to work, but she avoids to cross the busy main road.
content.corrected_sentence: Lisa prefers to walk to work, but she avoids crossing the busy main road.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I would like visiting the new art gallery downtown, so let's go there this weekend.
content.error_sentence: I would like visiting the new art gallery downtown, so let's go there this weekend.
content.corrected_sentence: I would like to visit the new art gallery downtown, so let's go there this weekend.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My parents love spend time in the garden on sunny afternoons, especially in summer.
content.error_sentence: My parents love spend time in the garden on sunny afternoons, especially in summer.
content.corrected_sentence: My parents love spending time in the garden on sunny afternoons, especially in summer.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My little brother hates ___ (eat) vegetables at dinner.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is used after 'would like/want', not after 'hate' as a general preference verb
bare base form cannot follow 'hates' — a gerund or to-infinitive is required, and only gerund is correct here
third-person singular present form is a verb, not a complement — it creates a grammatically broken structure
